description:
Ray is shutting down. We ended operations this week, and I'd rather share it honestly than let it trickle out.
...
Ray started as a browser built for games. Our running joke was that Chrome was for Boomers and Ray was for Play. Somewhere along the way we saw a bigger problem: the browser sits on top of everything you do online, and none of that context was actually working for you. So we pivoted, and spent the last stretch building Ray into a browser that used your own context to help you.
...
What still gets me is what a team of ten pulled off. We shipped a browser 30% faster than Chrome. We took our compile times from over 24 hours down to under one. We built what I still think is the best split screen anyone's done in a browser — I'm going to miss it when Ray stops working. Ten people did all of that. And more than 500,000 people downloaded Ray and played tens of millions of hours on it.
...
In the end we couldn't raise what we needed or find a home for it, and we ran out of road.
.

description: Getting acquired by Atlassian should come with a warning label. Loom was fast, clean, genuinely delightful. Now users are calling it the fastest post-acquisition decline they've seen. Rivaling what Salesforce did to Slack. Here's what always kills these products: the roadmap stops serving users and starts serving the parent's cross-sell goals. Every feature decision filters through "how does this drive Jira adoption?" instead of "how does this make recordings better?" The product doesn't get worse by accident. It gets worse on purpose. These companies acquire products because they can't build anything that good. Then they prove exactly why. Every botched acquisition reopens a market. # Post by Avani Solanki Prabhakar · LinkedIn · 2026-07-06

**Avani Solanki Prabhakar**: Chief People and AI Enablement Officer at Atlassian with 24 years 11 months of experience. Previous roles include Chief People Officer at Atlassian, SVP, Global Head of Talent & HR at Atlassian, and Vice President at Atlassian. Based in Australia [AU].

---

Here’s something cool that [Atlassian](https://linkedin.com/company/atlassian) and [Dropbox](https://linkedin.com/company/dropbox) have in common with our AI transformation journeys.

During the pandemic, both companies made deliberate decisions to set themselves up to work incredibly effectively while remote. That meant two things:
✔️ We had to drive a people-led, enterprise-wide transformation
✔️ We built a culture of async work: everything documented, communication mostly written (or Loom) and teams were open by default

The demos were never the hard part. Shipping something reliable enough to run in production was, and it still is.

description:
MacRumors reported Thursday that OpenAI has confirmed ChatGPT Atlas — its standalone AI browser for Mac, launched in October 2025 — will be shut down on August 9, 2026. That’s fewer than ten months after launch, which by any measure is a short run for a product OpenAI billed as a new interface paradigm: a browser that could carry out tasks on your behalf, not just surface information.
...
But read the fine print before calling this a retreat. OpenAI is not abandoning the ambition. The agentic browsing capabilities are being folded into a substantially upgraded ChatGPT desktop app — multiple tabs, password management, autofill, downloads, page search, passkeys, enterprise sign-in, ChatGPT Work, and Codex integration — and a new Chrome extension that adds highlighted-text actions, tab controls, access to local files, and persistent task management. Atlas users are being notified with transition details ahead of the deadline.
...
The Atlas shutdown is the most instructive counter-example
eventTitle: OpenAI Kills ChatGPT Atlas: Why the Browser Was Never the Real Battlefield - FourWeekMBA

description:
但两年过去，iOS生态的分裂不仅没有发生，《数字市场法案》催生的一系列第三方iOS应用商店反而撑不下去了。日前软件开发商MacPaw发布公告称，将于2026年2月16日关闭旗下iOS第三方应用商店Setapp Mobile，在这一截止日期后，用户通过Setapp Mobile安装的所有iOS应用将从设备中被移除、且无法使用。
...
此前在2024年春季，当苹果宣布为遵守《数字市场法》的规定，将通过iOS 17.4在欧盟实施多项变革，其中就包括第三方应用商店。消息一出MacPaw旋即响应，Setapp Mobile也成为了首个第三方iOS应用商店。
...
那么问题就来了，天时地利人和俱在的情况下，Setapp Mobile为何仅仅存续了不到一年半时间呢？答案其实是苹果棋高一着，用“核心技术费”（Core Technology Fee）掐住了第三方应用商店的咽喉。
...
MacPaw在支持页面中直言不讳地指出，第三方应用商店面临“仍在演变且复杂的商业条款”，是Setapp Mobile关闭的直接原因。按照苹果方面的规定，无论App Store、还是第三方应用商店分发的iOS应用，一款App每年安装量超过100万次后，开发者就需要每年为用户的第一次安装支付0.5欧元。
...
没错，苹果的核心技术使用费与游戏引擎Unity当初搞的按次收费一模一样，抬高了开发者使用第三方应用商店的成本，导致App越畅销反而越赔钱。如果App有内购服务，还需要使用苹果官方支付渠道（IAP），虽然此时苹果确实不会收取30%的抽成，但开发者也需要至少缴纳17%的费用。
...
这一套组合拳下来，知名手游登陆Setapp Mobile反而可能支付比App Store更多的费用，而不知名的App上Setapp Mobile，又很容易被埋没。没了“苹果税”、却多出“核心技术费”，而且还蹭不到App Store的影响力，如此一来，无论哪种体量的App上Setapp Mobile都没什么性价比。
...
Setapp Mobile最大的挑战就是用户的信任成本，导致它被苹果的“核心技术费”拖垮。反观“外部支付”，就既让开发者享受到了App Store的便利，又跳过了使用App Store要付出的代价，属于典型的“搭便车”，这也是为什么苹果在美国和日本为了取消外部支付而进行积极游说。

He acknowledged the reorganization was not as "clean" as it could have been, and that executives had "miscalculated on the timing" of the changes.
...

Meta laid off about 8,000 employees — roughly 10 percent of its global workforce of approximately 78,000 — in May, and simultaneously transferred approximately 7,000 employees into newly formed AI-focused teams, including the Applied AI Engineering unit and the Agent Transformation Accelerator. Together, those changes touched close to one-fifth of the company's total headcount.

I cannot stress this point enough. “Autonomy has to stay observable” is the line, and the session-visibility features are how they deliver on it. But observability you don’t configure or review is just logging nobody reads. Somebody has to decide who’s allowed to assign work to agents, which projects get the native coding agent turned loose, how session records are audited, and what the review gate looks like before an agent’s PR can merge. That “somebody” is going to be us. This is the part of the job that looks less like classic Jira admin and more like platform governance — and it’s the part I’d start skilling up on today.\n...\nThe through-line: the work is shifting from configuring a system for people to governing a system where people and agents work side by side. ---

🇺🇦🛡️ Ukrainian defence tech funding map 2026-2027

Ukrainian defence tech has grown more than twentyfold in funding over two years. But working out who gives money and whether Ukrainians can apply directly has become a task in itself. So we split donors into three tiers.

🇺🇦 Tier 1: national
🔹 Brave1: up to UAH 8M, up to UAH 30M/year, direct application
🔹 EU4UA Defence Tech: up to €150K, EU co-funded
🔹 Brave International: over €100M, joint 50/50 funds with partner countries

description:
MacPaw builds Mac and iOS software, best known for CleanMyMac, the utility that tidies and speeds up a Mac, and Setapp, a subscription bundling hundreds of paid apps for one monthly fee. Oleksandr Kosovan started it in 2008 from a dorm room at Kyiv Polytechnic, at age 21, with CleanMyMac as the single product. MacPaw is still headquartered in Kyiv, and it kept operating and shipping through the invasion, a fact the company has been open about.
...
Setapp is the growth engine now, and by mid-2025 it offered access to more than 260 Mac and iOS apps on one subscription. MacPaw has also pushed into two directions that matter in 2026: Moonlock, its cybersecurity line, and AI woven into existing products. A bootstrapped Kyiv utility maker that became a platform other developers distribute through is a quieter kind of success than a unicorn round, and a durable one.

description:
🚨 SAP shipped 200+ AI agents. 60% of migrating customers just left them on the shelf.
...
SAP spent Sapphire 2026 promising the autonomous enterprise. Then a survey of 200 large companies revealed the uncomfortable truth: most are walking right past the AI while they migrate.
...
A Horváth survey of 200 companies with 200M euro plus turnover found 60% of firms moving to S/4HANA are not adopting Joule at all.
...
Joule is included in RISE public cloud licenses. Basic functions come with private cloud too. It's built into S/4HANA with no separate implementation effort.
...
So this isn't a budget problem or a procurement fight. Companies are skipping something they already own.
...
The core migration is harder than teams expect, so everything else gets deprioritized. In the same survey, 46% said they would need more time and bigger budgets after go-live.
...
AI becomes the "phase 2" that quietly never arrives.
...
SAP's own CTO Philipp Herzig says to start Joule early, ideally in parallel

description: “Candidate Agent is an important step toward making hiring more intelligent, connected, and candidate-centered,” said Varun Kacholia, Cofounder and Chief Technology Officer at Eightfold AI. “This is not a generic chatbot bolted on existing legacy systems. Candidate Agent gives candidates one continuous, conversational experience across job discovery, application, scheduling, follow-ups, handoff into AI Interviewer and more. description:
The hard part of enterprise AI is not making models smarter. It is resolving a real tension between two forces that pull in opposite directions: the agency that makes an LLM useful, and the control that makes a workflow trustworthy. Over the past year, building the runtime behind Agentforce, we have organized our entire architecture around reconciling those two forces. We call the approach guided determinism. It rests on Agent Graph orchestration, the Agent Script language, specialized subagents, and fine-tuned routing models, all running across globally distributed Hyperforce infrastructure. This is what we learned building it.

description:
Enterprises deploying Claude Code and Claude Desktop across development teams need centralized control over access, cost, and policy. At scale, this is hard to manage: each developer needs an individual credential, settings must be distributed manually, and spend is difficult to track or cap. Without a centralized control point, governance is left to whatever tooling each team can implement independently.
...
Today, we’re announcing the Claude apps gateway for AWS, a self-hosted control plane that gives organizations a single point of control over access, cost, and policy for Claude Code and Claude Desktop. It replaces the need to provision a separate cloud credential per developer, push settings to every laptop by hand, or stand up separate tooling to track spend. You can deploy it through Amazon Bedrock to keep data within the AWS security boundary, or through Claude Platform on AWS to get the same gateway controls with the native Claude platform experience.

description: Until now, giving an AI agent access to credentials in a browser meant passing them as plain text into the agent's context, where they were directly accessible to the model. 1Password built a zero-exposure security framework that allows agents to use stored credentials in the 1Password vault without them ever reaching the model. Rather than forcing users to choose between sharing their credentials or manually authenticating, it gives agents exactly what they need, scoped to the task, with multiple layers of protection that ensure secrets remain encrypted until the moment they are injected into the page by 1Password. Access is granted per session and scoped to a specific set of approved items. That authorization does not carry over to other sessions, eliminating standing access. 1Password for Claude is the first integration built on it. description: The Model Context Protocol, commonly known as MCP, is an open standard originally introduced by Anthropic in November 2024. Its core purpose is to give AI models a standardized, vendor-neutral way to connect with external tools, data sources, APIs, and services. Before MCP, integrating an AI assistant with a business system required bespoke connectors — a fragmented, expensive, and time-consuming effort for every new tool or model combination. MCP resolves this by functioning essentially like a universal plugin system for AI: any application that exposes an MCP server can be accessed by any AI agent or assistant that supports the MCP client standard. It defines how AI systems can discover available tools, request their execution, and consume the results in a structured, secure, and interoperable way.\n...\nMicrosoft's endorsement of MCP is not peripheral — it reflects a deliberate strategy to make interoperability the foundation of its AI platform ambitions. In early 2025, Microsoft form

# Post by Piyush Gupta · LinkedIn · 2026-07-10

**Piyush Gupta**: Implementation Body Member at PEC ACM CSS with 2 years 9 months of experience. Previous roles include Member at PEC ACM CSS. Based in Pehowa, Haryana, India [IN].

---

Every AI has a memory. None of them share it.

So I built one that does.

The demo video shows the same conversation flowing between Gemini, ChatGPT, Perplexity, Claude and my terminal. Switching tools feels like continuing the conversation instead of restarting it.

That's what Recall does.

How?
A single SQLite database becomes the shared brain.
1. Every MCP compatible tool reads from and writes to it.
2. A lightweight browser extension connects ChatGPT, Gemini, Claude, and Perplexity.
3. Everything is local first.
4. Nothing leaves your machine unless you want it to.

The project is fully open source.

description: OpenAI has released a new ChatGPT desktop app for the Mac. Normally, an app release is relatively straightforward, but in this case, I feel as though I’m missing something. description: Google began rolling out Gemini Spark to the Gemini app on macOS in beta on June 30, 2026, letting the agentic assistant automate desktop tasks like file-sorting and spreadsheet-building for Google AI Ultra subscribers age 18+ in the US. The move signals Google now treats agentic assistants as an operating-system-level battleground rather than a chat feature, putting direct pressure on Claude Desktop and Microsoft Copilot as the default automation layer on knowledge workers' machines. The update adds native integrations with Google Tasks, Google Keep, Canva, Dropbox, Instacart, OpenTable, and Zillow Rentals, plus support for custom Model Context Protocol connections so users can wire in their own tools. The demand signal extends beyond vendors. In the first half of 2026 alone, at least three independent interchange proposals appeared: MIF, an individual developer’s memory interchange spec; memorywire, an academic wire-format proposal for memory operations; and the community-driven Open Memory Protocol, which describes itself as vendor-neutral. Add COGX, a vendor’s own export format, and you have four different answers to the same question in six months. description: Memory Belongs to the User: Portable Memory, an Open Standard Proposal for Cross-Vendor AI Memory · MacPaw Research\n\nAI assistants became systems that remember: preferences, projects, relationships, and history now persist across sessions and increasingly shape what these systems do for us. That memory is the most personal dataset a person delegates to software — and today it is locked inside each vendor's proprietary storage, in incompatible shapes, with no way to move it and no way to prove it has ever been deleted. We believe this is the data portability gap of the AI era, and that it should be closed the way the web closed it for documents, mail, and calendars: with an open format. This paper proposes Portable Memory, an open, vendor-neutral standard for carrying AI memory across applications, devices, and vendors — lossless, local, and with verifiable deletion as its guarantee of integrity. We present the vision and principles; an overview of the format (a self-contained`.mem` bun

description:
# Post by OJOBIT · LinkedIn · 2026-07-05

**OJOBIT**: OJOBIT is a deep tech research and engineering studio that helps teams convert high-friction problems into tested systems, clear operating workflows, and durable technical advantage. OJOBIT has 1-10 employees.

---

Espresso decodes 1.08 ms per token on an M3 Max, 4.76x faster than Apple's CoreML.cpuAndNeuralEngine path at 5.09 ms.

→ Espresso is a Swift library that compiles MIL programs directly to the Apple Neural Engine via reverse-engineered private APIs (ANEClient, ANEInMemoryModel) and moves data through IOSurface buffers with zero marshaling.
→ Fused triplet kernels pack 3 transformer layers into a single ANE dispatch, reducing 6 layers to 2 eval calls. KV cache lives entirely in IOSurface buffers, untouched by the CPU during decode.
→ The benchmark runs a 6-layer transformer with hidden dim 768, 12 heads, 32k vocabulary, sequence length 256 on macOS 15. Results are reproducible with the project's scripts.
→ Against llama.cp

→ Espresso is a Swift library that compiles MIL programs directly to the Apple Neural Engine via reverse-engineered private APIs (ANEClient, ANEInMemoryModel) and moves data through IOSurface buffers with zero marshaling.
→ Fused triplet kernels pack 3 transformer layers into a single ANE dispatch, reducing 6 layers to 2 eval calls. KV cache lives entirely in IOSurface buffers, untouched by the CPU during decode.
→ The benchmark runs a 6-layer transformer with hidden dim 768, 12 heads, 32k vocabulary, sequence length 256 on macOS 15. Results are reproducible with the project's scripts.
→ Against llama.cp
audit_reasoning: OJOBIT (technology company) LinkedIn post on Espresso Swift library performance vs CoreML; credible tech vendor signal directly tracking MACF-LOCAL (inference optimization on Mac).

...
If you’ve been building autonomous agents or heavy RAG pipelines locally on your Mac, you probably love Ollama. But if you’ve ever noticed those massive 10-15 second delays (Time-To-First-Token) when feeding your model a massive system prompt, you know the pain of CPU-GPU synchronization bottlenecks.
...

...

description:
For three years the correct answer to “Ollama or LM Studio?” was a shrug. Both wrapped llama.cpp, the reasoning went, so they generated tokens at the same speed and the only real difference was a command line versus a graphical app11. That was true, and then it stopped being true. In the year leading into mid-2026, LM Studio dropped its commercial-license requirement, Ollama built an MLX backend into its Apple Silicon path, and the two tools converged on the same feature surface they used to differentiate on. What is left to decide is not which inference engine you get. It is the developer surface, the license, and a ceiling neither tool advertises.
...
Until July 2
...
LM Studio is a desktop application first and a server second. Its strength is the part Ollama deliberately does not build: a graphical interface for searching Hugging Face, downloading GGUF or MLX checkpoints, loading several models at once, and chatting with them without writing a line of configuration. LM Studio 0.3.4
eventTitle: Ollama vs LM Studio: Picking a Local LLM Runtime in 2026 · Groundy
llm_synthesis:
For three years the correct answer to “Ollama or LM Studio?” was a shrug. Both wrapped llama.cpp, the reasoning went, so they generated tokens at the same speed and the only real difference was a command line versus a graphical app11. That was true, and then it stopped being true. In the year leading into mid-2026, LM Studio dropped its commercial-license requirement, Ollama built an MLX backend into its Apple Silicon path, and the two tools converged on the same feature surface they used to differentiate on. What is left to decide is not which inference engine you get. It is the developer surface, the license, and a ceiling neither tool advertises.
...
Until July 2
...
LM Studio is a desktop application first and a server second. Its strength is the part Ollama deliberately does not build: a graphical interface for searching Hugging Face, downloading GGUF or MLX checkpoints, loading several models at once, and chatting with them without writing a line of configuration. LM Studio 0.3.4
audit_reasoning: Groundy's Ollama vs LM Studio comparison identifies runtime ecosystem maturation and differentiation in 2026, directly supporting MACF-LOCAL competitive field tracking.

description:
During WWDC week — June 8 to 12, 2026 — Apple shipped a Swift class called`MLXLanguageModel` that barely made the keynote highlight reel. Point it at almost any of the roughly 4,800 models sitting inside Hugging Face's mlx-community organization, and your app talks to that model through the exact same Foundation Models API Apple uses for its own on-device system model. Tool calling, structured output, streaming — identical code path, different brain underneath.
...
Here’s the contrarian part: the format war for on-device Mac inference is already over, and nobody won it with a benchmark. A Hugging Face Space called mlx-my-repo won it by making the entry cost zero. No Python. No terminal. No local setup. You paste a model ID, you wait, you get a working MLX repo. That’s the whole workflow, and almost nobody outside the MLX community is writing about it.
...
mlx-my-repo isn’t a separate conversion engine. It’s a web front end wired to`mlx-lm`, the Python package the MLX team maintains for

description:
MLX is Apple's native machine-learning array framework, released December 2023 and developed by its machine-learning research group, designed to exploit the unified memory architecture of M-series chips where CPU, GPU, and Neural Engine share a single memory pool. Apple built it as a direct answer to PyTorch and TensorFlow treating Mac as an afterthought, forcing developers through layers of abstraction that ignored the hardware's most distinctive advantage. The framework arrived with immediate Metal Performance Shaders integration. Every operation compiles directly to Apple's GPU instruction set. Cross-platform code needs translation layers that slow it down. For local LLM users, inference is a memory-bandwidth problem. You're moving billions of quantized weights through the memory subsystem as fast as possible. Any inefficiency in that path directly subtracts from your tok/s.

**Denis Andreev**: Denis Andreev has held roles including Developer at Trade Planet LTD. Based in San Francisco, California, United States [US].

---

Mac malware is evolving rapidly, and PamStealer is a prime example of how attackers leverage native macOS features against users—especially on Apple Silicon devices. This malware masquerades as the legitimate clipboard tool Maccy, targeting users who download it from fake sites. Using AppleScript, it restricts execution to Apple Silicon Macs while excluding Eastern European systems, showcasing targeted, adaptive behavior.

description:
Apple has released an update to XProtect for macOS - MacDailyNews

Apple has released a new update to XProtect, its built-in malware protection tool, for all macOS versions.

This update bumps XProtect to version 5351 (from 5347) — the first broad release since early June — and adds fresh detection rules for several threats, including the Boatload stealer family and new osascript-based malware.

As usual, Apple provides no public details on the specific threats addressed. All users should check that the update has installed.

The Eclectic Light Company: ‎

This version adds six new Yara rules for MACOS.ANGRYORB.OX, MACOS.BOATLOAD.STCRST, MACOS.BOATLOAD.PRIN, MACOS.BOATLOAD.PEME, MACOS.VSHELL.DITA and MACOS.VSHELL.DR, amends rules for MACOS.MISOMAGIC.PA, and tidies spacing for those for MACOS.SOMA.STTX. From the rules for Boatload, that would appear to be a stealer.

Osascript rules in XPScripts.yr gain no less than 14 new rules, and amend those for MACOS.OSASCRIPT.TIPA, MACOS.OSASCRIPT

description:
Unlike much of the commodity stealer activity on macOS, which is built on AppleScript droppers or thin Objective-C wrappers, CrashStealer is implemented in native C++ around an internal class the authors named`MacOSData`. It validates the victim's login password locally before harvesting, collects broadly across browsers, cryptocurrency wallets, password managers and the keychain, encrypts what it collects with AES-GCM before exfiltrating over libcurl, and persists by copying and re-signing itself. Although its objectives overlap with families such as Atomic (AMOS), MacSync and Phexia, its native C++ implementation and client-side encryption set it apart, and we track it as a distinct family rather than a variant.

description:
When the local model cannot cope, Apple sends the request to Private Cloud Compute. This is not a normal cloud AI. Apple built it around five hard requirements: stateless computation, enforceable guarantees, no privileged runtime access, non-targetability, and verifiable transparency.
...
In plain terms, per Apple’s own Security Research documentation and independent breakdowns of it: your request is used only to answer you and is then deleted, with nothing written to disk and nothing kept in logs. Administrators cannot reach into a node and read your data, even during an outage. Your device refuses to talk to a server unless that server can cryptographically prove it is running the exact software Apple published. And Apple releases the PCC source code, in the`apple/security-pcc` project on GitHub, so outside researchers can check that the deployed system matches the promise.
...

description:
Here is what happened. Apple updated its App Store submission requirements. Starting September 2026, you cannot submit a new app or push an update without completing a revised set of privacy disclosure questions. Not partially completing them. Not skipping the sections that seem complicated. All of them, fully answered, before the submission goes through.
...
The first is that the questionnaire got longer. Apple built the original version before AI features became standard in consumer apps. The questions did not account for apps that send user conversations to a language model, use personal context to generate recommendations, or integrate third-party AI services that have their own data handling practices. All of that is covered now, and if your app touches any of it, there are specific new questions you have to answer.
...
The second is that incomplete answers now block submission. Previously you could sometimes get an update through gaps in your privacy section. From September onwar

The antitrust landscape has shifted for anyone planning a deal in healthcare, energy, housing, or agriculture. The Department of Justice (DOJ) and Federal Trade Commission (FTC) are prioritizing mergers and acquisitions that affect what every-day Americans pay. Healthcare alone accounts for 60% of merger challenges under the current administration, and the FTC's new Healthcare Task Force shows this focus will remain. Four things dealmakers in consumer-facing industries should be thinking about in this administration: • Heightened scrutiny applies regardless of deal size. • Healthcare deals are likely to face more coordinated review by various FTC bureaus. • Agencies will entertain robust divestiture packages that address competitive concerns, but early planning will be important. • Agencies will still litigate when they need to. [Damien Geradin](https://linkedin.com/in/damiengeradin) and I had the privilege of acting as counsel for the Coalition for App Fairness as an intervener in support of the Commission’s decision.

description:
Very interesting to see the CMA's new Steering Conduct Requirement consultation on Apple's App Store (published 30 June 2026) converge on a fee framework that closely echoes the principles I set out in "Regulating FRAND Access to App Stores under the DMA" (JCLE, 2026).
...
My article argued that FRAND platform access under DMA Article 6(12) needs four things:
...
1. gatekeepers shouldn't be paid for network-effect rents;
2. fees must reflect the reciprocal value developers create for the ecosystem;
3. pricing should account for the net value exchange between platform and developer, not just one direction; and,
5. costs should be assessed on a long-run incremental basis.
...
The CMA's proposed steering fee principles land in almost the same place: a cost-based principle built on forward-looking incremental cost, a value-based principle that explicitly requires "adjusting for the effects of Apple's substantial and entrenched market power" and accounting for "the value contributed by Deve

**Marco Scialdone**: Head Litigation & Academic Outreach at Euroconsumers with 5 years 3 months of experience. Based in Italy [IT].

---

The freshly delivered judgment of the EU General Court in the Apple case marks a watershed moment in the early enforcement history of the Digital Markets Act.

Apple sought nothing less than a declaration under Article 277 TFEU to render Article 6(7) of the DMA entirely inapplicable. description:
Happy to share a new blog post on the Kluwer Competition Law Blog (Wolters Kluwer: Antitrust and Competition Law), looking at the CMA's proposed Steering Conduct Requirements for Apple and Google published on 30 June 2026 under the DMCCA.
...
The post focuses on the Steering Fee framework, which the CMA structures around 3 principles:
...
1) a cost-based principle allowing platforms to recover genuine costs and a reasonable return rather than a share of developer revenue;
...
2) a value-based principle permitting a platform to capture a share of surplus where its offer is genuinely differentiated, subject to adjustments for market power and the two-way exchange of value;

3) an administrative simplicity principle guarding against fee structures that introduce unnecessary complexity or burden for developers.

description:
The European General Court has rejected Apple’s antitrust appeals against its “gatekeeper” classification under the region’s Digital Markets Act. The ruling upholds regulations requiring the tech giant to permit rival software interoperability within iOS and across its five digital App Stores. While Apple argued that the mandate compromises user privacy and security protections, European judges sided with regulators aiming to foster market competition.
...
The legal battle stems from the EU’s Digital Markets Act (DMA), a legislation designed to prevent tech monopolies from suffocating smaller competitors. Under these rules, regulators slapped Apple with a “gatekeeper” designation. This legally forced the iPhone maker to open up its software architecture and ensure competing hardware can easily communicate with its operating systems. Apple filed multiple lawsuits to strip away these labels, but European judges systematically dismantled every single argument the company presented.

description: Here is the correction worth making explicitly. The obligations that developers care about — allowing alternative app marketplaces and web distribution on iOS in the EU, letting developers steer users to cheaper payment options outside the App Store, and opening iOS features to third parties — do not originate in this ruling. They come from the DMA's gatekeeper rules themselves and from separate Commission enforcement, most notably the €500 million anti-steering decision of 23 April 2025, the first fine ever levied under the DMA, which found that Apple's restrictions prevented developers from directing users to cheaper options; Apple has appealed that fine, and it remains pending. Wednesday's ruling is a different matter from that appeal, and from the separate Commission interoperability proceedings and specification decisions on iOS access.\n...\nThe reality check is that the gap between the rules on paper and working access remains wide. There is one architecture that removes the third-party-disclosure problem, the retained-log problem, and the privacy-policy problem entirely: running an open-weight model locally, on hardware the firm owns and controls. “Local AI” (also called on-device or self-hosted AI) means downloading the weights of an open-weight large language model -- Meta’s Llama family, Mistral’s open models, DeepSeek’s V3/R1 releases, Alibaba’s Qwen family, OpenAI’s gpt-oss releases, and many others published on repositories such as Hugging Face-- and running inference entirely on a local machine using a runtime such as Ollama, LM Studio, or llama.cpp. The prompt never leaves the machine. No query is transmitted to any provider’s servers; no chat log is created on third-party infrastructure; no privacy policy or terms of use governs the interaction; and there is no provider to subpoena, no log to freeze under a litigat

There is one architecture that removes the third-party-disclosure problem, the retained-log problem, and the privacy-policy problem entirely: running an open-weight model locally, on hardware the firm owns and controls. “Local AI” (also called on-device or self-hosted AI) means downloading the weights of an open-weight large language model -- Meta’s Llama family, Mistral’s open models, DeepSeek’s V3/R1 releases, Alibaba’s Qwen family, OpenAI’s gpt-oss releases, and many others published on repositories such as Hugging Face-- and running inference entirely on a local machine using a runtime such as Ollama, LM Studio, or llama.cpp. The prompt never leaves the machine. No query is transmitted to any provider’s servers; no chat log is created on third-party infrastructure; no privacy policy or terms of use governs the interaction; and there is no provider to subpoena, no log to freeze under a litigat

description:
# Post by Risk Management · LinkedIn · 2026-07-10

**Risk Management**: Founded in 2021.

---

Risk Management Excel Dashboard Suite: https://lnkd.in/d8qwAahr
AI governance is not a policy document.

It is a continuous risk management discipline.

The NIST AI RMF helps organizations structure that discipline through four connected functions:

✓ Govern accountability, ownership, and oversight
✓ Map context, stakeholders, impacts, and dependencies
✓ Measure safety, reliability, privacy, fairness, and performance
✓ Manage treatment actions, residual risk, incidents, and monitoring

For generative AI, this also means addressing prompt injection, data leakage, synthetic content misuse, model drift, supplier dependencies, and explainability.

The real test is not whether an organization has AI principles. It is whether every AI system can be inventoried, risk-tiered, tested, monitored, governed, and evidenced.

How mature is your organization’s AI risk management approach?

#NISTAIRMF #AIGov

description:
The NCSC sorts generative AI into three tiers. Public LLMs are open to anyone over the internet, and the do-not-input rule applies to them in full. Cloud-provided private LLMs are bought under a business contract, with clearer terms on how your data is handled. Self-hosted models run on your own infrastructure and are realistic only for large organisations. The same product often spans two tiers.
...
ChatGPT Business, at around $25 per user per month, states there is no training on your business data by default, and Claude’s Team plan at similar money adds admin controls and keeps ownership of prompts with the firm rather than individual staff.
...
The tier does not change the mindset. Even on a business plan, prompts and documents leave your environment. The sensible sequence is to experiment on public tools with nothing sensitive in play, then move any workflow that touches organisational data onto a contracted business plan, where you can check the data processing agreement, control

description: The global standards for AI governance that U.S. companies must follow are no longer merely voluntary suggestions. The EU AI Act (Regulation 2024/1689) entered into force on August 1, 2024, with fines of up to €35 million or 7% of annual worldwide turnover — exceeding the GDPR. Three frameworks now set the baseline: the EU AI Act, ISO/IEC 42001:2023, and the NIST AI Risk Management Framework 1.0. This guide explains what each requires, which applies to U.S. companies, and how to build a program that satisfies more than one at a time.\n...\nProhibitions on unacceptable-risk AI became enforceable on February 2, 2025. Under the Digital Omnibus provisional agreement of May 7, 2026, most high-risk Annex III obligations are now deferred to December 2, 2027. However, the prohibited-practice bans and GPAI rules are already enforceable and unchanged. Companies that wait until December 2027 to start will miss the documentation and

description:
# Post by Paddle · LinkedIn · 2026-07-14

**Paddle**: Paddle is a merchant of record solution for B2B & B2C digital companies, helping increase global conversions, reduce churn, stay compliant, and scaling up fast. Paddle has 300-400 employees (+8-9% YoY) and has annual revenue in the GBP20M-GBP30M range. Headquartered in London, England, United Kingdom, operates in 20 countries (including Portugal, Canada, United States, Netherlands, and Spain). with 11 prior funding rounds.

---

In a world where anyone can build, some app founders are getting existential.

No, the "APPocalypse" isn’t here just yet. But AI is quietly reshaping how consumer apps get valued, priced, and acquired.

For most subscription businesses, the numbers are stronger than ever. Leaner teams, lower OPEX, better margins.

But underneath that, harder questions are being asked.

Volume-based models capture more spend but they make LTV murkier, cohorts harder to read, and acquirers more cautious about what comes next.

description:
# Post by AI Builders Hub · LinkedIn · 2026-07-03

**AI Builders Hub**: Founded in 2025.

---

New data shows who is really winning the consumer AI race.

It is not as simple as “ChatGPT is winning.”

And it is not as simple as “Claude is catching up.”

The real story is more interesting.

Sensor Tower’s State of AI 2026 report shows three apps dominating consumer AI:

ChatGPT.
Google Gemini.
Anthropic Claude.

ChatGPT still owns reach.

It has more than 1.1B monthly users, compared with Gemini at 662M and Claude at 245M.

So yes, ChatGPT is still the scale leader.

But scale is only one side of the race.

The more important signal for builders is this:

Claude is monetizing its smaller audience better.

According to reported Sensor Tower data, Claude’s US mobile ARPU reached $2.76 in May 2026, compared with ChatGPT at around $1.74.

Claude also leads major AI assistants on paid conversion, with around 13% of users paying for a subscription.

That changes the narrative.

ChatGPT built

description:
Anthropic is about to test whether consumers will pay cloud-style usage fees for a frontier chatbot. Starting July 12 at 11:59 PM PT, subscribers to Anthropic's $20, $100 and $200 monthly Claude plans will need to pay additional usage-based fees to access Claude Fable 5, WIRED's Max Zeff reported in a WIRED story.

description: TL;DR (July 2026): On July 6, 2026 the free preview of ChatGPT Workspace Agents ended. Every agent run inside a Business, Enterprise, or Edu workspace now draws down workspace credits on top of the per-seat fee you already pay. The published GPT-5.5 rates: 125 credits per million input tokens, 12.50 per million cached input tokens, 750 per million output tokens - a typical end-to-end run lands around 5 to 25 credits. What OpenAI did not publish is a consumption table for real workflows, so every team that automated prospecting, reporting, or ticket routing during the free spring now has a variable operating cost it cannot budget from the vendor's own docs. Here's a sharp, opinionated take on what b9940 *really* means for production users in the agent race.

`ggerganov/llama.cpp` just shipped b9940. On the surface: more macOS builds. Under the hood: a quiet but telling regression.

ASICs are application-specific integrated circuits that can be customized to make them more efficient

description: SaaS is dead. Long live SaaS. Nobody was ever buying just code, which is why the obituary got filed early. AI writes software now, multiples compressed, and every application company got repriced as if a customer with an AI subscription is a customer about to churn. Code has been close to free at the margin for decades. You could always fork an open source project or hire a contractor. Companies mostly didn't, because the code was the least of what they were paying for. The contract buys a counterparty. Someone else carries the uptime, the security patches, the compliance certs, the integrations that break when a partner changes their API. Someone else answers the phone when the thing falls over during quarter close, which is when things fall over. description:
# Post by TTL Capital · LinkedIn · 2026-07-04

**TTL Capital**: TTL Capital has 1-10 employees, founded in 2025. Headquartered in Accra, Greater Accra Region, Ghana.

---

The benchmark on your board deck now fails 4 out of 5 public SaaS companies.
Rule of 40 — growth rate plus profit margin should clear 40 — became the default health check for software. In Q4 2025 the median public SaaS score was just 28%. Only about 20% of names cleared the bar (17 of 60).

Here's the part most founders skip: how you count "profit" decides the whole story. On an EBITDA basis, only 15% of public SaaS clear 40. On a free-cash-flow basis, 46% do. Same companies, same quarter — three times the "winners" depending on which line you pick.

Private is harder. Median score of 12%: roughly 10% growth, 6% margin. And the cause isn't overspending — margins have actually improved since 2022. Growth is what fell off.

So treat the Rule of 40 as a composition test, not a scoreboard. A 40 built on 35% growth and 5%

description:
July 9 was one of the densest single days in AI product history: OpenAI launched GPT-5.6 (Sol/Terra/Luna), ChatGPT Work, merged Codex into the desktop app, and lost its No. 2 executive in the same 24-hour window. The week's broader signal is a market that's moved from "who has the best model" to "who can most efficiently run AI at enterprise scale" — as Palo Alto's CEO put it, token pricing must fall 90% before the full enterprise opportunity unlocks.
...
Trend: The race has shifted from model capabilities to workflow embedding. GPT-5.6 and ChatGPT Work are the clearest example yet of an AI lab turning a model release into an enterprise product launch.

description:
# Post by Kingscrowd · LinkedIn · 2026-07-03

**Kingscrowd**: Kingscrowd has 20-30 employees (+20% YoY) and has annual revenue in the under $1M range, founded in 2018. Headquartered in Binghamton, New York, United States. Has $12.2M in total funding, with 14 prior funding rounds.

---

In this week’s Chart of the Week, Kingscrowd updated our equity crowdfunding revenue multiple benchmark for 2026, looking at companies that disclosed both valuation and at least $50K in annual revenue.

The headline: the median revenue multiple for companies that raised between June 2025 and June 2026 was 20.5x, slightly above the 20.1x median from last year’s edition.

That matters because multiples had been declining steadily since 2021. The hoped-for continued reset did not really happen.

The good news: valuations become much more reasonable as companies grow. Startups generating more than $1M in annual revenue had a median revenue multiple of 8.4x.

Revenue multiples are not the whole diligence proc

description: On July 1, San Francisco-based Together AI closed an $800 million Series C at an $8.3 billion post-money valuation, one of the largest AI infrastructure financings of the year and more than a doubling of the roughly $3.3 billion valuation the company carried at its $305 million Series B just sixteen months earlier. The round was led by Aramco Ventures, the venture arm of the Saudi oil giant, with participation from Vista Equity Partners, General Catalyst, Emergence Capital, Nvidia, March Capital, Pegatron, and SentinelOne’s S Ventures. According to the company’s announcement, Together AI intends to expand its inference platform, launch new products, and scale its compute footprint by approximately fifty-fold over the next five years.\n...\nWhat turns this raise into a signal rather than just a headline is what Together AI actually sells. The company is a neocloud: it rents Nvidia GPU clusters and AI-specific infrastructure and gives businesses a way to train and deploy open-weight models
eventTitle: Together AI Raises $800 Million at an $8.3 Billion Valuation as Enterprises Abandon Closed Models for Open Source Infere

description: GitHub and Salesforce are running AI pricing experiments for all of us to learn from. One of them repriced for the customer, the other repriced to survive. GitHub's version was desperate. In April they stopped accepting new paid signups entirely. Agentic coding blew up their compute costs, reportedly doubling weekly spend, and every new flat-rate customer made the math worse. They chose zero new revenue over two more months of that. June 1, they launched token billing for everyone. Devs posted projections of $29 a month becoming $750. TechCrunch's headline: "What a joke." Signups only started reopening mid-June. Salesforce ran its pricing pain in public instead. Agentforce launched at $2 per conversation. Adoption was tepid, implementation folks called it a non-starter. So they shipped credits at 10 cents an action. Then flat enterprise agreements, one negotiated fee, unlimited use. Their CRO's own words: 'we agree on a flat fee, and then it's a shared risk.' Three models in 18 months,
eventTitle: GitHub and Salesforce AI Pricing Experiments Yield $1.2B ARR | Peep Laja posted on the topic | LinkedIn

On June 25, 2026, Azeem Azhar’s Exponential View published the State of the AI Economy — the first publicly available, bottom-up, deduplicated measure of generative-AI commercial revenue. The headline figures: $110 billion in booked revenue over the trailing twelve months, and an annualized run rate now exceeding $175 billion. The ~1.6× gap between those two numbers is not rounding error — it is the encoded growth velocity of the market in real time.
...
The methodology matters as much as the numbers. The dataset covers app-layer, foundation-model, and infrastructure-hosting revenue across 1,000-plus companies. It is deduplicated — so API revenue that flows through an app is not double-counted at the model layer — and it is global ex-China. Critically, it excludes chip manufacturing: this is a software and services number, not an Nvidia hardware figure. When people cite the $175B, they need to carry that asterisk.

description: I'm going to confess, I don't fully understand what Atlassian were thinking with Opsgenie. Back in June 2025 they emailed their customers to say that Opsgenie would reach end of life in 2027, and that they should move to either Compass or Jira Service Management — a move that opened the door for every customer to ask whether they're really working with the best product and team in the space. And so on April 5, 2027, it's gone for good, and you need to be migrated somewhere else. We built our Opsgenie Rescue Program to take the sting out of the process, making it easy to upgrade to an on-call product that's purpose built for modern engineering teams. We'll provide our services free of charge during the overlap with your existing contract, so you're never paying twice, import your existing setup rather than rebuilding it by hand, and back it all with up to 99.99% uptime. Having been through plenty of software migrations in my career, I know nobody loves them. This one may be unavoidable,

description:
# Post by Edmund Munday · LinkedIn · 2026-07-12

**Edmund Munday**: Principal Product Manager - CI CD & Ecosystem, Bitbucket Cloud at Atlassian with 17 years 6 months of experience. Previous roles include Senior Product Manager - CI CD & Ecosystem, Bitbucket Cloud at Atlassian, CEO & Co-Founder at Need2Know.io, and Senior Product Manager Tribe Lead - Shared Functionality, Integration Automation Ecosystem at StarRez, Inc.. Based in Melbourne, Victoria, Australia [AU].

---

Most AI features ship, demo well, and never make a dollar.

After years shipping AI and platform products, the gap I see usually isn't model quality. It's that teams treat "we added AI" as the finish line — when it's actually the starting line for a monetization problem.

The features that turn into revenue tend to clear four gates, in order:

1. Job, not feature. The AI has to own a job the user was already paying for — in time or money — not add a novelty button. If you can't name the job, you're not selling anythi

description:
Diia.City Growth: Boosting Ukraine's IT Sector Amid Challenges

The Ministry of Digital Transformation has issued a statement defending the special legal regime of Diia.City, responding to recent public criticism. The agency emphasized that this project remains a key driver of the development of Ukraine’s IT sector, continuing to attract business even in wartime conditions.

This is reported by Business • Media

## Thousands of companies and hundreds of thousands of specialists in Diia.City

According to the Ministry, there are currently about 4,500 companies holding the status of Diia.City residents, which unite over 175,000 specialists. The agency specifically highlights the significant role of Diia.City in the development of defense technologies: since the beginning of the full-scale war, over 520 companies and startups specializing in defense-tech have joined the space.

description: In under two years, Workday bought six AI companies. Most customers I work with have never seen the map in one place, so I drew it. 2024 was point solutions: HiredScore for talent intelligence, Evisort for contract intelligence. Folded into the record. Then the second half of 2025, over about fourteen weeks: Paradox for frontline hiring at roughly a billion, Flowise for building agents, Sana for the knowledge front door at about 1.1 billion, Pipedream for the wiring into every other system. That is not a shopping list. That is an agent platform assembled in one quarter. The through-line is a move from system of record to system of action. For twenty years Workday's moat was holding the authoritative record. The uncomfortable truth behind two billion in spending is that the record alone is no longer a moat, so Workday bought the front door and the ability to act. Four of the six have shipped. Two are still integration promises with dates. That difference matters when the roadmap slide b

Workday is moving beyond simple AI features (like reporting insights or recommendations) toward task-executing agents that can:
...
·       Trigger workflows (hire, approve, terminate, reclassify roles)
...
·       Answer employee and HR questions conversationally
...
·       Auto-generate job descriptions or compensation ranges
...
·       Detect anomalies in payroll, finance, or workforce data
...
·       Recommend actions (not just insights)
...
These are often embedded inside Workday HCM, Financials, and Recruiting workflows rather than being separate “chatbots.”
...
Think of them as “process assistants” inside enterprise workflows:
...
2. Recruiting Agent
...
·       Drafts job descriptions
...
·       Shortlists candidates based on historical hiring patterns
...
·       Schedules interviews automatically
...
Why this is a big shift
...
AI-enabled Workday = system of action
...
That means:
...
·       Less navigation through modules
...
·       More natural language → workflow exe

description: HONG KONG SAR - Media OutReach Newswire- 2 July 2026 - Jamf, the standard in managing and securing Apple at work, has announced general availability of AI Governance, a new capability within Jamf for Mac that enables IT and security teams to discover actively-used AI tools, enforce policy controls, and generate audit-ready reporting. This move makes Jamf first-to-market to deliver native, OS-level AI governance controls for Mac. Many organizations struggle to confidently audit and report on AI tool usage across their device fleet, including both sanctioned applications and unsanctioned or prohibited tools. AI Governance provides comprehensive visibility into which AI applications are in use, along with detailed insights into how they behave on the endpoint. This enables organizations to understand AI activity at a level that network- and cloud-based reporting solutions alone cannot provide, helping security teams identify risk, support compliance, and make informed governance decisions

description: Why enterprises pay for it. Simple math. A company has maybe 50 engineers who can build agents in code and 5,000 employees who understand the business processes worth automating. Low-code is how you close that gap. Google's centerpiece announcement at Cloud Next 2026 was the Gemini Enterprise Agent Platform with Agent Studio for low-code development and an upgraded Agent Development Kit for code-first teams, and Microsoft, Salesforce, and ServiceNow have made the identical bet. When every hyperscaler ships the same product shape in the same year, that's not a trend, that's a procurement category.\n...\nWhat to study. n8n for the open-source workflow layer (this is the one I'd learn first, because it teaches you workflow-as-graph thinking that transfers everywhere). And underneath all of them, learn the pattern: triggers, deterministic orchestration,

It came from the unglamorous stuff. Governed data models. A semantic layer that turns "weekly active users" into one definition instead of five conflicting ones. Encoded workflows. Validation systems that check the outputs.
...
This changes the whole enterprise knowledge conversation. For years, the pitch was "connect to everything, understand it, act on it," and that promise carried a premium. But if the real leverage lives in your own semantic definitions and a handful of encoded workflows, the buyer's question shifts.

Second, the jump from 21% to 95% is the cost, not the freebie. Building the governed datasets and the semantic layer is exactly the slow work most companies underfund. Skipping it is part of what these platforms actually sell.
...
So the takeaway isn't that AI replaces your data platform. It's closer to the opposite. The model layer is commoditizing. The connector layer is commoditizing. The defensible ground is the governed semantic layer underneath, and the trust

description:
For years, agents had amnesia — every session started from zero. Copilot Studio's Memory changes that: the agent keeps persistent, per-user context across interactions, learning a person's preferences and patterns over time.
...
1. Decide what Memory should store — and what it must not. Preferences and working patterns, yes. Sensitive personal details it doesn't need, no. Write the list down.
...
2. Set a retention period. Persistent doesn't mean forever. Decide how long a remembered preference lives before it expires — and set it before go-live, not after.
...
3. Wire the deletion path. Under GDPR and the EU AI Act, a person can ask to be forgotten. You need a working way to erase what Memory holds about them — tested, not theoretical.
...
4. Isolate strictly per user. One person's remembered context must never surface in another's session. Confirm the boundary holds.
...
5. Log what's stored. Treat Memory as a system of record — you should be able to answer "what does the agent know

# Apple WebKit · LinkedIn · 2026-07-02

## Author

**Apple WebKit**

*Official LinkedIn account for the WebKit Open Source Project — the web browser engine that powers Safari and other apps on macOS, iOS, iPadOS, visionOS, Windows, and Linux.*

## Post

Agents are great at writing code, but they don't know how the browser renders it. The Safari MCP server changes that. Connect it to a live Safari window and your agent can check the DOM, read console output, inspect network requests, and take screenshots on its own. No more describing what you're seeing. It can check it out for itself. Available now in Safari Technology Preview 247. Works with Claude, Codex, and any MCP-compatible client.

## Top comments

- Edgar Quintanilla: Been waiting on this one! Can’t wait to use it with my a11y agents!

## Engagement

- Reactions: 16
- Comments: 1

description:
If you’ve ever watched an AI coding assistant forget everything you told it five minutes ago, you understand the specific flavor of frustration that drove Alex Newman to build claude-mem. The open-source plugin, which gives Anthropic’s Claude Code a persistent memory layer, racked up roughly 46,000 GitHub stars in about 48 hours after launching in early April 2026.
...
Claude Code, like most AI coding assistants, is fundamentally stateless. Every session starts fresh, with the model having zero recollection of your previous conversations, decisions, or debugging rabbit holes.
...
Claude-mem tackles this by capturing data from coding sessions, including tool calls and session events, then generating semantic summaries that get stored in a local SQLite database. When you start a new session, the plugin automatically injects relevant historical observations so Claude already has context about your project.
...
The installation is a single command: npx claude-mem install. No cloud accounts

description: The short answer: No single RFC-level standard exists for AI agent memory engrams as of mid-2026. The closest things are the Model Context Protocol (MCP) — an open protocol from Anthropic that standardizes how applications expose context to LLMs — and the Engram Specification (Apache-2.0), an open format published by PLUR that defines the data structure for portable agent memory. Together they address the transport layer and the data layer, but neither has achieved IETF-level standardization. Glaze by Raycast turns a chat into a real Mac app — and hit 1 on Product Hunt with 494 upvotes – Top AI Product

July 3, 2026

## Glaze by Raycast turns a chat into a real Mac app — and hit 1 on Product Hunt with 494 upvotes

Raycast, the team behind the keyboard launcher power users swear by, just shipped Glaze. You describe the app you want in plain language, and the AI builds a real desktop app — icon in your dock, launches instantly, no browser tab pretending to be software.

## What it actually is

Not a code generator that hands you a repo. Glaze produces a native Mac app that runs offline and reaches into your machine: local files, CLIs, keyboard shortcuts, menu bar, background processes. It can also hit any external API or pull in AI models, so a two-sentence prompt can become a working tool wired to the services you already use. Don’t like a button? Keep chatting, or point-and-annotate the exact element to change. Ship it to the public store or keep it private for your team.

**Ray Fernandez**: Journalist at Moonlock with 19 years 8 months of experience. Previous roles include Journalist at The Daily Upside, Journalist at Techopedia, and Journalist at TechRepublic. Based in Argentina [AR].

---

Fake site for the Pearcleaner app is pushing a Mac stealer

If you recently downloaded the Pearcleaner app on your Mac, there is a chance your computer was breached by a stealer. Pearcleaner is a popular open-source free Mac app that helps users delete apps on their computers. A fake site impersonating it has popped up, and security vendors have still not caught up to the threat.

The technique that cybercriminals are using in this campaign is a classic template that’s reused over and over. In other words, even if you did not download this particular app, this report is a must-read, as a solid understanding of this method could save you a lot of grief in the future.

description:
Apple has released an update to XProtect for all macOS – The Eclectic Light Company

Apple has just released its first update to XProtect for all versions of macOS since early June, bringing it to version 5351; the previous version was 5347. As usual it doesn’t release information about what security issues this update might address.

This version adds six new Yara rules for MACOS.ANGRYORB.OX, MACOS.BOATLOAD.STCRST, MACOS.BOATLOAD.PRIN, MACOS.BOATLOAD.PEME, MACOS.VSHELL.DITA and MACOS.VSHELL.DR, amends rules for MACOS.MISOMAGIC.PA, and tidies spacing for those for MACOS.SOMA.STTX. From the rules for Boatload, that would appear to be a stealer.

Osascript rules in XPScripts.yr gain no less than 14 new rules, and amend those for MACOS.OSASCRIPT.TIPA, MACOS.OSASCRIPT.SYPR, MACOS.OSASCRIPT.TADE and MACOS.OSASCRIPT.REOB.

You can check whether this update has been installed by opening System Information via About This Mac, and selecting the Installations item under Software.

A full listing

# Post by Konstantin Klyagin · LinkedIn · 2026-07-05

**Konstantin Klyagin**: Founder at Redwerk with 31 years 5 months of experience. Previous roles include Senior Software Developer at Nokia, Developer at Open Source Community, and Periodical contributor and columnist at Software Wydawnictwo. Based in Lisbon Metropolitan Area [PT].

---

In March, a founder pinged me about his iOS app being stuck in App Store review for weeks. He had built it on Replit, vibe-coded the whole thing, quite proud of how fast it went from idea to live demo.

The demo genuinely looked good. Smooth, functional, the kind of thing that used to take months. He was ready to ship. Then Apple went quiet on him.

I dug into what actually happened. Turns out Apple didn't ban vibe coding. It enforced Guideline 2.5.2, a rule that has existed since the App Store launched: apps must be self-contained. They cannot download or execute code that changes their own functionality.

What Replit does is run your generated app
eventTitle: Post by Konstantin Klyagin

# Anthony Dawes · LinkedIn · 2026-07-02

## Author

**Anthony Dawes**

## Post

Today, the Court of Justice of the European Union upheld in full a September 2022 General Court judgment which, in turn, had essentially upheld a July 2018 decision in which the European Commission established that, in order to strength and protect its general search service, Google had abused a dominant position by: (i) requiring manufacturers to pre-install the Google Search app and browser app (Chrome), as a condition for licensing Google's app store (the Play Store); and (ii) preventing manufacturers wishing to pre-install Google apps from selling even a single smart mobile device running on alternative versions of Android that were not approved by Google (Android forks). Congratulations to everyone involved on the European Commission side, including most recently Fernando Castillo de la Torre Carlos Urraca Caviedes, Brice Allibert, João Vareda, Massimiliano Kadar and Gunnar Wolf

description:
The AI Act was built to catch high-risk systems before they reach the market, and the regulation the Council adopted on 29 June moves that obligation from August 2026 to December 2027 for stand-alone systems and to August 2028 for those built into products.
...
What using a "giving more time to comply" framing makes one miss is how postponement interacts with non-retroactivity under Article 111. A system placed on the market before the new date falls under the obligations only if it's substantially modified, so moving the date lets high-risk systems deployed before the new date run outside the obligations as long as they're not substantially changed. A system placed on the market before December 2027 but operating after it can sit outside the Act indefinitely unless altered after that date.
...
Tld;dr: Because the AI Act doesn't apply retroactively, the delay works as a standing exemption for everything deployed in the 2026-2028 window that it opens.
...

description: The EU AI Act and GDPR are complementary layers of European law, not alternatives: the AI Act regulates the AI system — how it is built, classified by risk, and monitored — while GDPR regulates the personal data flowing through it, and where an AI system processes personal data both apply at once. They overlap heavily on impact assessments, human oversight, transparency, risk management, and documentation, so a single well-built control can discharge duties under both. But the AI Act adds obligations GDPR never had — risk classification, conformity assessment, and post-market monitoring of the system itself — which means you can be fully GDPR-compliant and still breach the AI Act. Real tension exists too, most sharply between the AI Act demand for representative training data and GDPR data minimisation, and it must be resolved by governance rather than left to two separate teams. Both regimes bind the organisation deploying the AI, not just the vendor. The efficient answer is one unifi

description:
On 2 August 2026, the EU AI Act enters a new phase — though not the one many organisations have been preparing for. Transparency obligations under Article 50 take effect, and the AI Office and national authorities gain full powers to enforce and sanction breaches, including for general-purpose AI (GPAI) models. What doesn’t happen on this date, following a legislative change adopted this summer, is the broad rollout of high-risk AI obligations that was originally scheduled.
...
- GPAI enforcement becomes operational — the substantive obligations for providers of general-purpose AI models (Articles 51–55) have applied since 2 August 2025. What changes now is that the AI Office can act on its enforcement and sanction powers over those providers, including investigations and administrative fines.
...
- High-risk AI systems (Annex III) are, notably, not part of this date anymore. The EU’s “Digital Omnibus,” which amends the AI Act, received final approval from the Council on 29 June 2026.

description:
The European Union's top data protection authority adopted new rules Wednesday that formally close a compliance assumption tens of thousands of organizations have built their data practices around: that stripping identifying fields from a dataset makes it anonymous for GDPR purposes. The European Data Protection Board's Guidelines 02/2026 on Anonymisation replace the previous working definition with a three-test standard that regulators say reflects the real capabilities of modern AI — capabilities that have made the old field-stripping approach legally unreliable.
...
The EDPB's framework tests data against three cumulative criteria. Data must resist individual isolation — no specific person's record can be singled out. It must resist linkage — it cannot be connected to other datasets in ways that enable identification. And it must resist inference — probabilistic or AI-powered derivation of attributes about specific individuals from the data must be prevented.

description:
Everyone read Legora's switch to consumption-based model as a pricing update. I read it as the first legal AI vendor shifting its own compute bill to the customer.
...
For 3 years, the vendor ate that risk. You paid a flat number, you used the tool as much as you wanted and if your usage spiked, that was the vendor's problem to absorb.
...
Consumption pricing shifts that.
...
Consumption is a more accurate way to price software that thinks for itself. A quick lookup and an agent grinding across 10,000 contracts are not the same amount of work and charging one flat number for both was always a loss leader.
...
Last week I spent a call teaching a procurement team what a single AI search actually consumes. They had no idea because they've been sold unlimited everything, told the price on the page was the price and left to assume usage was somebody else's problem.
...

This shift reflects Anthropic's strategy to widen what analysts call a "price umbrella," charging different prices to different customer segments for the same underlying technology.

Apple is in talks with a small Silicon Valley company that says it can shrink powerful artificial intelligence models enough to run directly on an iPhone, the startup's CEO told CNBC. PrismML, a Khosla Ventures-backed spinout from the California Institute of Technology, publicly released compressed versions of Alibaba's open-source Qwen model on Tuesday. The company said it reduced the model from roughly 54 GB to less than 4 GB, allowing all 27 billion of its parameters to run on an iPhone 15 or newer. PrismML CEO Babak Hassibi told CNBC that Apple and other companies have been evaluating the startup's models and measuring their speed, energy efficiency and performance on devices.

description: Quietly but significantly, Apple has shifted one of the most important trust boundaries in consumer AI infrastructure. In a new expansion of Private Cloud Compute, Apple said it will run some Apple Intelligence workloads on Google Cloud, marking the first time PCC has operated beyond Apple-owned data centers, according to the Apple Security Blog and reporting from InfoQ. The move matters because PCC was created to avoid ordinary cloud trust assumptions and replace them with verifiable guarantees, as Apple explained in its earlier Private Cloud Compute architecture post. By transplanting that model onto third-party infrastructure, Apple is testing whether confidential AI can remain credible when the operator, hardware stack, and physical facilities are no longer fully under one vendor’s control.\n...\nApple’s move is strategically clever because it expands AI capacity without asking users to accept a lower-trust privacy model. Apple said it used technologies behind Google’s Gemini family

description: Boards are now scheduling security reviews around AI model releases, not incidents. That shift showed up clearly in Omdia's Q1 2026 Cybersecurity Titans Index. Palo Alto Networks fielded over 1,200 customer strategy review requests after the latest frontier model launches. Tenable held hundreds of advisory calls. CrowdStrike launched Project Quiltworks. The trigger in each case was a model release, not a breach. Each major release now compresses sales cycles, and we expect this to be the single biggest demand catalyst in the second half. description: SaaS Revenue Multiples June 2026: Trends and Insights What’s the median revenue multiple for publicly traded SaaS companies, how has it evolved, and why? As of June 30th, 2026, the median revenue multiple was 3,93x, which is: 👉 35% lower YoY (6,06x at the end of June 2025), but rebounded 20% from the record-level low of 3,27x in the middle of April 2026 👉 79% lower from all-time high (19.04x on Dec 18, 2020) 💡 Why the drop since 2020? It’s all about slower growth. The median YoY revenue growth rate for SaaS companies is now 20%, near the lowest levels since 2014 and far from the +30% YoY long-term growth in the last two decades until the end of 2022, which fueled higher multiples in previous years. ⚖️ Key takeaway: Slower growth = lower multiples. So, unless revenue growth will significantly increase, there is no coming back to valuations (revenue multiples) seen during the Tech Bubble (H2-2020 to H1 2022). description:
# Post by TiDB, powered by PingCAP · LinkedIn · 2026-07-02

**TiDB, powered by PingCAP**: TiDB is a distributed SQL database built for AI agents. They provide vectors, transactions, and analytics in one engine with ACID guarantees and elastic scale. TiDB, powered by PingCAP has 300-400 employees (+10% YoY) and has annual revenue in the $1M-$10M range, founded in 2015. Headquartered in Sunnyvale, California, United States, operates in 21 countries (including China, Singapore, Japan, India, and Malaysia). Has $341.6M in total funding, with 5 prior funding rounds.

---

Atlassian's Forge platform uses a one-schema-per-tenant model. Every customer gets their own isolated schema. At Atlassian's scale, that means over 3 million tables - all inside a single TiDB cluster.

The engineering write-up covers what it took to get there: the metadata bottlenecks, the TiDB internals that had to evolve, and the performance results on the other side.

Full breakdown 👉 https://lnkd.in/giH3WNYk

#TiDB #At

description:
Mark Zuckerberg told Meta employees something the industry's marketing has spent two years denying: AI agents aren't progressing on schedule. At an internal town hall on July 2, 2026, first reported by Reuters, he said the "trajectory of the agentic development over at least the last four months hasn't really accelerated in the way that we expected," and that the company's bets on its restructured organization "haven't come to fruition yet."
...
The admission lands hard because of what preceded it. In May, Meta laid off about 10% of its global workforce and reassigned roughly 7,000 employees to AI-focused teams — moves premised on the belief that agents would absorb the work. Zuckerberg said executives planning the reorg in January and February were "super optimistic about tools like Claude Code." Meta is projected to spend up to $145 billion on AI infrastructure this year, part of Big Tech's $700 billion-plus outlay — spending we've tracked from the capex side and the bubble side.
...

description:
The booking system is currently in a state of constant change. After the recent update of the rules in May 2026, introduced by Cabinet Resolution No. 692, the booking procedure may undergo further changes. The main ideology of the changes is the transition from formal "criticality" to real economic indicators, in particular through the level of employees' salaries. As of July 2026, the number of booked persons in Ukraine exceeded last year's figures by 300 thousand, which forced the government to review sectoral and regional criteria.
...
The salary criterion for enterprises remains unchanged. As before, to confirm the status of critically important, the average salary must be at least three minimum wages (for enterprises in combat zones - 2.5 minimum wages).
...
At the same time, the draft establishes a new obligation: by August 10, 2026, enterprises must submit a certificate of average salary and a tax calculation for the last calendar month. Failure to comply with these requirements
eventTitle: Last chance to keep booking until August 10: businesses have only one month to confirm salaries - sud.ua

description:
The EU is opening funding instruments for Ukrainian companies working in dual-use technologies for the first time. At the Ukraine Recovery Conference (URC) in Gdańsk, the European Commission announced the launch of two new mechanisms under the Ukraine Facility. €340 million in guarantees and grants are expected to help mobilize up to €700 million in investment for the dual-use sector. Combined with already existing programs, the total support package reaches €500 million and has the potential to unlock up to €1.1 billion in financing.
...
Until recently, the Ukraine Facility instrument was associated primarily with reconstruction. However, the European Commission's latest decisions demonstrate a broader approach: support for the economy is becoming increasingly linked with the development of defense capabilities.
...
The new programs will be implemented by the French state investment bank Bpifrance and the Finnish export credit agency Finnvera. They will combine loans, grants, and equi

description: with the US government, we’ve updated our cybersecurity safeguards. The vast majority of coding work is unaffected. In the near term, the new safeguards will flag a slightly higher fraction of harmless requests than the previous Fable safeguards; we’re working to refine these over the coming weeks. Users will be clearly notified when a request is flagged, and they’ll instead receive a response from Opus 4.8. Our biology and chemistry classifiers are unchanged from our initial launch. These are still broader than we would like; they trigger fallbacks to Opus 4.8 on basic biology-adjacent questions. Improvements to these classifiers are landing soon. The update also introduces integrations with Google Keep, Tasks, Canva, Dropbox, Instacart, OpenTable, and Zillow Rentals alongside Model Context Protocol (MCP) support for custom code links. Furthermore, the assistant gains real-time tracking capabilities across sports, finance, and news sites.
...
Google is officially expanding the reach of its always-on autonomous AI assistant by launching a Gemini Spark app on macOS alongside a massive suite of external app integrations. Available exclusively for premium Google AI Ultra subscribers, the update transitions the software from a standard conversational window into a functional desktop agent. The launch aims to streamline heavy workflows by letting the artificial intelligence interact directly with local files, cloud platforms, and live internet feeds simultaneously.
.

description:
Frontier labs are competing on economics as much as capability, with OpenAI scaling intelligence on demand and SpaceXAI pricing repository-scale engineering as a commodity. The security and identity gaps are getting named and addressed, from Anthropic's Zero Trust blueprint to Vercel giving every subagent its own revocable identity. And the infrastructure underneath is being abstracted away, whether that's Meta opening a million-token model through an API, Microsoft erasing the GPU virtual-machine tax, or Figma and OpenAI killing latency in creative and voice workflows.
...

- A current threat map covering prompt injection, tool poisoning, identity and privilege abuse, memory poisoning, and supply chain attacks.
- A three-tier framework (Foundation, Advanced, Optimized) mapped to organizational maturity and risk tolerance, plus an eight-phase i

description:
One thing he highlighted that landed with the room was agentgateway, which had just joined the AAIF as its fourth hosted project five days before the summit. Agentgateway is an open source gateway built specifically for agentic AI traffic: it unifies MCP connections, agent-to-agent communication, LLM inference, and REST/gRPC services under one control plane. Contributors include AWS, Microsoft, Cisco, Adobe, and Apple. The point Nalley was making is that the governance question and the infrastructure question are now being answered together. The protocol has a neutral home in the Linux Foundation, and the operational tooling needed to run it in production is following.

**Stephen Kettley**: Stephen Kettley has held roles including Software Engineer at Asuer, Data Scientist at Botle Buhle Brands, and Data Science Fellow at Elucidate AI. Based in City of Johannesburg, Gauteng, South Africa [ZA].

---

One of the most useful tools I’ve come across is Raycast.

At first, it just replaced the default Mac spotlight for me, but over time, it has become a powerful way to control my system, interact with apps, and connect directly into tools I already use.

What really makes Raycast stand out is the Raycast Store, which is packed with extensions for a huge range of apps and services. It turns your launcher into a central hub for productivity. Definitely check out their store! It has over 300 pages of interesting apps and utilities to connect to and use. Check it out here: https://lnkd.in/daJYcNeT

description:
Start with the mechanics, because the marketing word "memory" hides them. Each Claude Code session begins with a fresh context window. Two disk-backed mechanisms carry anything across sessions, and both are re-loaded at launch. CLAUDE.md files are loaded in full at the start of every session, walked up the directory tree and concatenated. Auto memory (a`MEMORY.md` the agent writes for itself) loads only its first 200 lines, or 25KB, whichever comes first; topic files load on demand and stay machine-local, not shared across machines or teammates.
